# 3. SHORT NOTES (QUICK REVISION)

## Introduction to Linked List
- **Linked List:** Data structure in which elements are linked using pointers.
- **Node:** Basic unit of a linked list consisting of data and a reference (link) to the next node.

## Types of Linked Lists
- **Singly Linked List:** Each node has only one reference (link) to the next node.
- **Doubly Linked List:** Each node has two references (links) to the previous and next nodes.
- **Circularly Linked List:** The last node has a reference to the first node.

## Operations on Linked List
- **Insertion:** Adding a new node at the beginning or end of the list.
- **Deletion:** Removing a node from the list.
- **Search:** Finding a specific node in the list.
- **Traversal:** Visiting each node in the list in a particular order.

## Linked List Operations
- **Insertion at the Beginning:** Creating a new node and linking it to the head node.
- **Insertion at the End:** Creating a new node and linking it to the last node.
- **Deletion at the Beginning:** Removing the head node.
- **Deletion at the End:** Removing the last node.

## Time and Space Complexity
- **Insertion:** O(1) for insertion at the beginning, O(n) for insertion at the end.
- **Deletion:** O(1) for deletion at the beginning, O(n) for deletion at the end.
- **Search:** O(n) for searching an element in the list.
- **Traversal:** O(n) for traversing the list.

## Advantages of Linked List
- **Efficient Insertion and Deletion:** Linked list allows efficient insertion and deletion of nodes.
- **Dynamic Memory Allocation:** Linked list uses dynamic memory allocation, which makes it memory-efficient.
- **Flexibility:** Linked list can be implemented in a variety of ways.

## Disadvantages of Linked List
- **Memory Usage:** Linked list requires more memory as each node needs to store a reference to the next node.
- **Slow Search:** Linked list has a slow search time as it requires traversing the list.
- **Complex Implementation:** Linked list has a complex implementation compared to arrays.

## Applications of Linked List
- **Database Systems:** Linked list is used in database systems to manage data.
- **Compilers:** Linked list is used in compilers to manage symbols.
- **File Systems:** Linked list is used in file systems to manage files and directories.

## Real-World Examples
- **Browser History:** Browser history is an example of a linked list where each node represents a webpage.
- **Undo and Redo:** Undo and redo features in text editors and word processors use linked list.
- **Playlist Management:** Playlist management systems use linked list to manage songs.
